criteria.label: Name and explain the six core RL components: agent, environment, state, action, reward, policy.
criteria.example: In CartPole: agent = controller; environment = physics simulator; state = pole angle and cart position; actions = push left/right; reward = +1 per balanced step; policy = rule mapping observations to left/right.

criteria.label: Contrast reinforcement learning with supervised learning in one concrete scenario.
criteria.example: Email spam: supervised uses labeled spam/ham emails; RL would only apply if the system chose actions (e.g., quarantine rules) and received delayed rewards (user complaints or false-positive reports)—not the same problem.

criteria.label: Implement or describe epsilon-greedy exploration on a 3-arm bandit and interpret results.
criteria.example: Run 1000 pulls with ε=0.1; print estimated values [0.31, 0.68, 0.49] and note arm 1 (true rate 0.7) was selected most often after sufficient exploration.

criteria.label: Sketch the agent–environment loop and write the discounted return formula.
criteria.example: Draw agent → action → environment → (state, reward) → agent; write G_t = r_{t+1} + γ r_{t+2} + γ² r_{t+3} + … with γ = 0.99 for a long-horizon task.

criteria.label: Explain how RLHF maps LLM training onto the agent–reward–policy paradigm.
criteria.example: Policy = GPT weights; state = prompt + context; action = generated reply; reward = score from preference model; PPO increases probability of high-reward replies after SFT initialization.
